_meta.source_notebook: Talkin Flag: Global Insights from the Gridiron (e70d736e-e7f2-4360-97b6-0948f10e16c9)
_meta.grounding: Built off the general 'Flag Football IQ — Core' bank. ~10 universal rules questions kept, the rest are coach-grade: scheme, situational decision-making, evaluation/development, officiating edge cases, and safety/leadership. Correct answers trace to official rulebooks (NFL FLAG / IFAF), route-tree & coverage references, and established coaching consensus (periodization/taper, motor-learning feedback, heat-safety). EVERGREEN — no time-sensitive standings or trivia.
_meta.status: DRAFT authored 2026-06-25. Owner (Ambra & Tika) should confirm each correct_index before this quiz formally counts toward coach voting influence. Answer key is server-only (RLS no-policy + service role).
_meta.scale: score_pct (0-100) is the published Coach IQ. For verified coaches it is the primary input to coach voting influence (see src/lib/eval/coachWeight.ts). Non-coaches still get a Coach IQ number with no poll weight.
_meta.fields_note: Each question carries optional `domain` and `tier` for future analytics. The iq_questions table has no column for these, so the seed script does NOT persist them — they live here as documentation/source-of-truth for a later analytics migration.

quizzes.title: Coach IQ — Flag Football
quizzes.description: A coach-grade test: rules a staff must master, scheme and situational decision-making, evaluation, development, officiating edge cases, and safety. Evergreen — sourced from the Talkin Flag research library.

questions.prompt: In NFL FLAG 5v5, how far behind the line of scrimmage must a defender start to rush the quarterback?
choices: 3 yards
5 yards
7 yards
10 yards
questions.explanation: A rusher must begin a minimum of 7 yards off the line of scrimmage, marked by the referee. The restriction lifts once the ball is handed off or pitched backward.

questions.prompt: Once the QB hands off or pitches the ball backward, what happens to the 7-yard rush restriction?
choices: It rises to 10 yards
It stays for the whole play
It is eliminated — any defender may cross the line
Rushing becomes illegal
questions.explanation: After a handoff or backward pitch the 7-yard restriction is lifted and any defender may cross the line of scrimmage — a key trigger for run-blitz timing.

questions.prompt: What is a 'No-Run Zone' in 5v5, and why must a coach plan for it?
choices: The end zone, where running is banned
Areas ~5 yards before the midfield line-to-gain and before each end zone where the offense must pass
Any spot within 7 yards of a rusher
The 5-yard line where conversions start
questions.explanation: No-Run Zones sit ~5 yards before the midfield line-to-gain and ~5 yards before each end zone; if the ball is spotted inside one, the offense must throw. They prevent short-yardage power runs and reshape goal-line and short-to-gain play-calling.

questions.prompt: In 5v5, can the player who receives the snap (the QB) advance the ball across the line of scrimmage themselves?
choices: Yes, anytime
No — the QB cannot advance the ball past the line; it must move by handoff, pitch, or pass
Only inside a No-Run Zone
Only on fourth down
questions.explanation: The QB cannot run it across the line. The ball advances via handoffs, backward pitches, and forward passes; another player must carry it across — central to how 5v5 run concepts are built.

questions.prompt: What is 'flag guarding,' and what is the typical penalty?
choices: A defender illegally covering a receiver; loss of down
A ball-carrier illegally obstructing access to their flags; usually a 5–10 yard penalty and often loss of down
Guarding the end zone on defense; a safety
A legal stiff-arm; no penalty
questions.explanation: Flag guarding is the ball-carrier illegally blocking access to their flags (arm/hand over the belt, lowering a shoulder, swatting). It typically costs 5–10 yards from the spot and often a down — a habit coaches must drill out of ball-carriers.

questions.prompt: Which statement about being 'down' and fumbles is correct in flag football?
choices: A dropped ball is a live fumble anyone can recover
If any body part other than hands/feet touches the ground the runner is down, and a ball that hits the ground is dead — there are no live fumbles
Only a flag pull can end a play
Fumbles may be returned for touchdowns
questions.explanation: A runner is down when an elbow, knee, etc. touches the ground; once the ball hits the ground the play is dead. No live fumbles means no scramble drills for loose balls — coaching emphasis shifts to clean exchanges and secure catches.

questions.prompt: Which best describes Cover 1?
choices: A pure zone with three deep defenders
Man coverage with one deep safety (and typically a rusher)
Two deep safeties splitting the field
An all-out blitz with no deep help
questions.explanation: Cover 1 pairs defenders man-to-man with a single deep safety over the top, usually freeing one rusher — sound when you trust your cover players and want pressure.

questions.prompt: Which best describes Cover 3?
choices: Man with one deep safety
Two deep safeties
Three deep defenders each guarding a deep third, with no rusher
An all-out blitz
questions.explanation: Cover 3 is a pure zone — three deep defenders each guard a third and no one rushes, blanketing the deep field. It trades a rusher for deep security.

questions.prompt: In a conventional numbered route tree, which is generally true?
choices: Even numbers break inside, odd numbers break outside (9 = the vertical go)
Even numbers break outside, odd break inside
All deep routes are even-numbered
Numbers indicate yardage only
questions.explanation: By common convention even-numbered routes break inside and odd-numbered routes break outside, with 9 the vertical go. A shared route-tree language lets a staff install and adjust concepts quickly.

questions.prompt: What is the rule on pre-snap motion in 5v5?
choices: No motion is allowed
Only one player may be in motion, moving parallel to or backward from the line — not toward the line at the snap
Any number of players may move forward
Only the QB may motion
questions.explanation: Only one offensive player may motion at a time, moving parallel or backward; moving toward the line at the snap is illegal. Legal motion is also a coach's best tool to diagnose coverage.

questions.prompt: A defense plays a two-deep zone (two safeties splitting the deep halves). Where is it most naturally stressed?
choices: The two deep sidelines
The deep middle / seam between the two safeties, and the short flats
Nowhere — it has no weakness
The line of scrimmage only
questions.explanation: Two-high zones leave the deep middle between the safeties and the short flats vulnerable — vertical seams and high-low flat/corner combinations are classic answers.

questions.prompt: Against pure man coverage, which concept most reliably creates separation?
choices: Everyone runs a deep go route
Crossing / mesh and rub concepts that force defenders to navigate traffic
Four players stand still
All hitches at the line
questions.explanation: Man defenders must chase their assignment, so crossing/mesh and legal rub concepts create natural traffic and separation — a staple man-beater.

questions.prompt: If a defender lines up with clear inside leverage on your receiver, the higher-percentage route generally breaks…
choices: Inside, into the defender's leverage
Outside, away from the defender's leverage (e.g., an out or corner)
It doesn't matter where it breaks
Straight at the defender
questions.explanation: Attack away from leverage: against inside leverage, breaking outside (out/corner) wins; against outside leverage, work inside (slant/post). Teaching receivers to read leverage is core route IQ.

questions.prompt: What best describes 'match' (pattern-match) coverage?
choices: Pure man with no help
Zone landmarks that convert defenders into man coverage based on how the receivers distribute their routes
An automatic blitz
Coverage that only applies on fourth down
questions.explanation: Match coverage starts with zone rules but pattern-matches: defenders carry receivers man-to-man once routes declare, blending zone vision with man tightness.

questions.prompt: Showing a two-high safety look pre-snap and rotating to one-high at the snap is primarily intended to…
choices: Save the defenders' energy
Disguise the coverage and corrupt the QB's pre-snap read
Comply with a substitution rule
Signal a timeout
questions.explanation: Late rotation disguises the true coverage so the QB's pre-snap math is wrong — a high-leverage way to bait throws without extra rushers.

questions.prompt: Your offense leads late; the opponent has no timeouts left. After a completion in bounds, the best clock-management priority is to…
choices: Spike the ball immediately
Keep the ball in bounds and let the play clock and game clock run
Throw deep down the sideline
Call your own timeout
questions.explanation: With a lead and the opponent out of timeouts, staying in bounds bleeds the clock. Sideline throws and out-of-bounds plays only help the trailing team.

questions.prompt: You trail late on offense with one timeout. To maximize remaining clock, you should favor…
choices: Routes that finish in the middle of the field
Routes that get the receiver out of bounds, saving the timeout for a critical down
Running as much clock as possible between snaps
Kneeling to regroup
questions.explanation: Trailing, the clock is the enemy: sideline/out-of-bounds routes stop it for free, letting you bank your lone timeout for a key down.

questions.prompt: Pushing tempo (a fast no-huddle) primarily benefits the offense by…
choices: Giving defenders more rest
Limiting the defense's ability to substitute, communicate, and disguise coverage
Forcing the referee to speed up spots only
Guaranteeing more rushing yards
questions.explanation: Tempo denies the defense time to sub, signal, and disguise — they're forced into vanilla looks the offense can attack.

questions.prompt: Backed up near your own end zone in 5v5, the dominant risk a coach should script away from is…
choices: Scoring too quickly
A loss/turnover deep in your own territory that yields a short field or a safety
Throwing too many short passes
Using motion
questions.explanation: Deep in your own end the cost of a sack, turnover-on-downs, or safety is highest — favor high-percentage, ball-secure concepts to escape the zone before taking shots.

questions.prompt: In standard 5v5 with four downs to reach the midfield line-to-gain, sound series management on 3rd-and-long is usually to…
choices: Always take a low-percentage deep shot on 3rd down
Play the series — take the high-percentage throw and keep 4th down live to reach the line-to-gain
Punt (kick the ball away)
Spike it to stop the clock
questions.explanation: With four downs to the line-to-gain you can manage the series across 3rd and 4th down rather than forcing a low-percentage 3rd-down shot; there is no punting in standard 5v5.

questions.prompt: A defense rushes two and drops the rest. For the offense, the immediate implication is…
choices: There are extra defenders rushing, so protect longer
Fewer defenders are in coverage, so a quick, decisive read of man/zone leverage beats the pressure
The QB may now run across the line freely
Receivers must stop their routes
questions.explanation: Two rushers means the coverage is thinner; a quick release to the leverage advantage (hot/quick game) defeats the rush before it arrives.

questions.prompt: Why is pre-snap motion one of a coach's most valuable diagnostic tools?
choices: It always draws a penalty on the defense
A defender traveling with the motion man signals man coverage; static defenders suggest zone
It adds a rusher
It stops the play clock
questions.explanation: Motion forces the defense to reveal itself: a defender chasing the motion man indicates man, while defenders staying in their areas indicate zone — informing the QB's read and the call.

questions.prompt: What is the primary purpose of a scripted period (a pre-planned sequence of plays) at the start of practice?
choices: To tire players out before drills
To guarantee priority situations and installs get rehearsed reps under controlled, game-like conditions
To avoid having to call plays in games
To let players choose the plays
questions.explanation: Scripting ensures your most important concepts and situations are actually repped each practice instead of being crowded out — it makes installs intentional and measurable.
questions.source_citation: Coaching consensus — practice design & install scripting

questions.prompt: Applying periodization as a major tournament approaches, a common best practice is to…
choices: Sharply increase total volume the final week
Taper — reduce training volume while maintaining intensity so players peak
Stop practicing entirely for a week
Add a brand-new offense the day before
questions.explanation: A taper (lower volume, maintained intensity) sheds fatigue while preserving sharpness so athletes peak for competition — a well-supported periodization principle.

questions.prompt: When projecting a young receiver to a higher level of play, which trait tends to be most transferable?
choices: Raw season touchdown totals against weak competition
Football IQ / processing and separation quickness — how they read leverage and create space
Jersey number
Height alone
questions.explanation: Counting stats are context-dependent on competition; processing speed, route IQ, and separation quickness travel across levels — which is why the evaluation taxonomy weights them heavily.

questions.prompt: During an individual skill rep, the most effective coaching feedback is generally…
choices: A long lecture covering five corrections at once
Specific, immediate, and focused on one key correction (often an external cue)
Silence until the end of practice
Only praise, never correction
questions.explanation: Motor-learning research favors specific, immediate feedback on one focal point, and external cues (effect on the environment) over internal cues — players adjust faster and retain more.

questions.prompt: A defender pulls a receiver's flag before the receiver has possession of the ball. How should this be ruled?
choices: The receiver is down at that spot
It is not a legal 'down' — a flag pull only applies to a ball-carrier; impeding the catch is illegal contact / pass interference
Automatic turnover
Replay the down with no penalty
questions.explanation: You can only be downed by a flag pull once you are a ball-carrier. Pulling a flag (or making contact) before the catch that prevents reception is illegal contact / defensive pass interference, not a down.

questions.prompt: You believe the officials misapplied a rule. Which is generally true about protests?
choices: Any call, including judgment calls, can be reversed on protest
Judgment calls (e.g., spot, pass interference) are not reviewable, but a misapplication of a rule can be raised with the head referee per the rulebook's procedure
Protests are never allowed
You must leave the field to protest
questions.explanation: Judgment calls are final; a genuine rule-application error can be addressed with the head referee through the prescribed protest/appeal procedure. Knowing the distinction keeps a coach credible.

questions.prompt: At a hot summer tournament, the best heat-illness practice for a coach is to…
choices: Withhold water to build toughness
Schedule hydration breaks, watch for heat-illness symptoms, and modify or pause activity in extreme conditions
Practice hardest at midday
Ignore it unless someone collapses
questions.explanation: Proactive hydration, acclimatization, symptom monitoring, and activity modification in extreme heat are the accepted standard — athlete safety is non-negotiable.

questions.prompt: Right after a player makes a costly mistake, the most effective sideline response is usually to…
choices: Bench them for the rest of the game without explanation
Give a specific, calm correction and reset focus to the next play
Say nothing and hope it improves
Single them out loudly in front of the team
questions.explanation: A specific correction plus a 'next-play' reset keeps the athlete confident and learning; public blow-ups erode trust and performance. Culture and communication are part of coaching credibility.

questions.prompt: On defense, what is the legal way to stop the ball-carrier?
choices: Strip or punch the ball loose
Pull a flag — stripping, punching the ball, tackling, holding, or pushing through the runner are prohibited
Wrap and bring them to the ground
Hold the jersey until help arrives
questions.explanation: Defenders must pull a flag; stripping/punching the ball, tackling, holding, and bull-rushing the runner are illegal. Coaches must drill clean, contact-free flag-pull technique.

questions.prompt: Why is 5v5 built around quick timing throws, short routes, and option routes?
choices: Deep passes are illegal in 5v5
The compact field and the rush count reward a quick release and built-in answers to coverage
There are no defenders downfield
Only the center may catch passes
questions.explanation: Tight spacing and an arriving rush make the quick game and option routes (built-in answers vs. leverage/coverage) the backbone of efficient 5v5 offense.
